results.possibleIdentity: Herbert (Herb) Aach (1923–1985) was an American painter, writer, and color theorist known for his pioneering work with fluorescent pigments and color engineering. Born in Cologne, Germany, he immigrated to the United States in 1938 to escape Nazi persecution. After serving in the U.S. Army during World War II, he studied at the Brooklyn Museum Art School under artists like John Ferren and Rufino Tamayo, and later in Mexico City. Aach is particularly recognized for his deep engagement with color theory; he translated and edited a notable edition of Goethe's "Theory of Colours" (1971) and developed a signature style known as "Color Expressionism." He was highly active during the 1960s and 1970s, producing major series such as "Precession of the Equinoxes" and "Split Infinity." Aach was a member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a collaboration that aligned with his technical background in pigment formulation (honed while working at the Art Crayon Company). His involvement with E.A.T. included working on projects for the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, where he applied his expertise in fluorescent paints. He was also a respected educator, teaching at Queens College from 1966 until his death in 1985.

results.possibleIdentity: Michael David is a prominent American theatrical producer and arts administrator who was a key figure in experimental theater during the late 1960s and 1970s. From 1969 to 1978, he served as the executive director of the Chelsea Theater Center in Brooklyn (based at the Brooklyn Academy of Music), alongside artistic director Robert Kalfin. Under his leadership, the Chelsea Theater Center became a hub for avant-garde and technologically integrated productions. In 1971, he facilitated a residency for the video collective Video Free America (led by Arthur Ginsberg), which resulted in landmark multimedia theatrical works such as Allen Ginsberg's 'Kaddish' (1972) and Peter Handke's 'Kaspar' (1973). These collaborations are often cited in the context of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, as they focused on the intersection of artistic performance and technical engineering (specifically early video technology). David later went on to co-found the successful producing partnership Dodger Theatricals (originally the Dodger Theater) in 1978 at BAM.

Michael David (born 1954) is a well-known American abstract painter primarily recognized for his mastery of the encaustic (wax-based) painting technique. While his professional career reached national prominence in the early 1980s, he was active as a student and emerging artist in the mid-1970s. He first encountered encaustic painting in an undergraduate course at the Parsons School of Design in 1975. His work often incorporates iconographic symbols and a physical, aggressive approach to the medium that he likens to the energy of the 1970s New York punk scene, in which he also participated as a bass player for the band the Plasmatics. His work is held in the permanent collections of major institutions, including the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the Guggenheim Museum, and the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA). He currently operates M. David & Co., a gallery and residency program in Brooklyn.

results.possibleIdentity: Marilyn Wood (1929–2016) was a prominent American choreographer, dancer, and intermedia artist celebrated for her pioneering site-specific performances known as "Celebrations." Her career began in the late 1950s as a member of the Merce Cunningham Dance Company (1958–1963), where she performed with influential figures such as John Cage and Robert Rauschenberg. In 1968, she founded "Marilyn Wood and the Celebration Group," a collaborative collective featuring dancers, architects, filmmakers, and musicians dedicated to transforming urban environments into performance spaces. Her most famous work, "Celebrations in City Places: The Seagram Building" (1972), utilized all 44 stories of the Mies van der Rohe-designed skyscraper, involving 35 dancers and multimedia elements like film projection and original music. Her work is historically associated with the experimental art movements of the late 1960s and early 1970s, including the milieu of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), as her "intermedia" approach emphasized the collaboration between art, technology, and community participation. Wood later established the International Center for Celebration (ICC) in 1987 in Santa Fe, New Mexico, to continue her work on environmental and cultural festivals globally.

results.possibleIdentity: James Evans was an artist and a figure in the mail art movement who was active from the early 1970s through the late 1980s. He is specifically associated with the Los Angeles chapter of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). His involvement with E.A.T. is documented in his personal collection, which is now housed at the Getty Research Institute. This collection includes archival materials and records related to E.A.T. Los Angeles, an organization that, while operating largely independently of the New York-based E.A.T. founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg, shared the goal of fostering collaborations between artists and engineers. Evans' work and collections reflect a broader engagement with the experimental art scenes of the late 1960s and 1970s, particularly those focused on network-based and technology-interfacing artistic practices.

John Evans (1932–2012) was a prominent New York-based artist active in the late 1960s and 1970s, primarily known for his conceptual collage work and participation in the international mail art movement. He began a daily collage project in 1964, which he continued for 36 years, creating a visual diary of everyday life in the East Village using found ephemera and mixed media. Evans was a peer of other major figures in the 1960s avant-garde, such as On Kawara, who sent him postcards as part of his 'I GOT UP' series in 1968 and 1969. While primarily a collage artist, his work was part of the same conceptual and communicative network that informed organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

Robert J. Evans was a museum director, curator, and art historian who documented and supported artists associated with the technological and collaborative movements of the late 1960s. He served as the Director of the Danforth Museum of Art in Massachusetts and was a curator for the Illinois State Museum. In 1978, he authored the introduction for a retrospective exhibition catalog for sculptor Virginio Ferrari, an artist who was a participant in the landmark 1968 Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum. Evans’ scholarly work in the 1970s focused on the intersection of modern sculpture, technical materials, and the evolving relationship between the artist and institutional spaces.

results.possibleIdentity: Don Little (also referred to as Donald Little) was an engineer associated with Bell Laboratories (Bell Labs) during the mid-to-late 20th century. Given his position at Bell Labs, which was the primary source of technical expertise for the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, he is a likely candidate for the technical collaborators E.A.T. sought to pair with artists. Records indicate he was involved in technical research related to wireless technology and radio systems, and he has been cited in Bell Labs-affiliated presentations alongside other engineers such as Jim Kile and Samir Shah. His activity aligns with the E.A.T. era of the late 1960s and 1970s, during which Bell Labs staff were frequently recruited for artistic collaborations.

Donald Little is a prominent musician and professor of tuba who has been active since the late 1960s. He earned his Bachelor of Music Education from the Peabody Conservatory in 1970 and his Master of Music from Northwestern University in 1971. He served as the principal tubist for the Fort Worth Symphony Orchestra and the Dallas Opera Orchestra for over 45 years. Notably, he has held artistic leadership roles within the Tubists Universal Brotherhood Association (T.U.B.A., now I.T.E.A.), an organization founded in 1973 dedicated to the artistry of the tuba and euphonium. His career as a performing artist and artistic director fits the time frame of being active in the late 1960s and early 1970s.

Don Little was a technical professional and head of an Instrumentation and Photography section at a research establishment during the 1960s and 1970s. Oral history records from the Canadian War Museum describe him as managing a team of 'instrumentation photography' specialists who were responsible for measuring the physical effects of experiments, such as blast impacts on structures. This role combined high-level photography with scientific measurement and technology, a multidisciplinary focus characteristic of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) community.
